summary: Raymond Castillo, a Senior Superintendent with Meridian Construction Group, testified regarding balcony slab waterproofing issues encountered during a construction project. He acknowledged being aware of the membrane application problems by August 2022 but did not document the defects properly, as required by his role. Despite admitting that schedule pressures led to early application of waterproofing, Mr. Castillo failed to file a non-conformance report and verify the corrective actions. He also noted discrepancies regarding his awareness and reporting of the issues, with conflicting statements about the timeline and verification of remediation work. Furthermore, he did not agree with characterizations implying defective subcontractor installation.

keyIssues.title: Balcony slab waterproofing issues
keyIssues.description: Witness was aware of problems around August 2022 regarding membrane application before concrete cured.

keyIssues.title: Responsibilities of Superintendent
keyIssues.description: It was part of his duties to verify that identified defects were corrected.

keyIssues.title: Awareness of Waterproofing Issue
keyIssues.description: He observed an installation issue with waterproofing in August 2022.

keyIssues.title: Schedule Pressure
keyIssues.description: Schedule pressure was a factor in the early application of waterproofing.

keyIssues.title: Remediation Work Performed
keyIssues.description: The work was necessary and was performed.

keyIssues.title: Deficient Documentation
keyIssues.description: Did not write a non-conformance report or verify remediation.

keyIssues.title: Failure to Document Known Installation Defect
keyIssues.description: Did not document a known installation defect with a non-conformance report for the first time.

missingInformation.description: Mr. Castillo, can you clarify the procedure Meridian Construction Group follows for documenting and reporting construction defects, particularly involving waterproofing issues?
Why was there a delay between June 2022 when the RFI was issued and August 2022 when you acknowledged awareness of the issue?
What specific schedule pressures influenced the decision to apply waterproofing prematurely, and how were these pressures communicated to the team?
Can you provide more detail on why no non-conformance report was filed despite recognizing the waterproofing defect as required by your responsibility?
Describe the decision process for validating that remediation work was completed if you did not personally verify it.
To what extent did payment records influence your conclusion that the remediation work was carried out correctly?
Please elaborate on the communication between you and Project Manager Tom Brennan regarding the initial observation and reporting of the waterproofing defect.

flags.text: Admission: Witness was aware of problems around August 2022 regarding membrane application before concrete cured.
Admission: It was part of his duties to verify that identified defects were corrected.
Admission: He observed an installation issue with waterproofing in August 2022.
Admission: The work was necessary and was performed.
Admission: Did not write a non-conformance report or verify remediation.
Admission: Did not document a known installation defect with a non-conformance report for the first time.

disclaimer: This output is not legal advice and requires attorney review before any action is taken.

depositionAnalysis.witnessRole: Senior Superintendent with Meridian Construction Group
depositionAnalysis.location: Whitaker & Ruiz, P.A., 1450 Brickell Avenue, Suite 2200, Miami, Florida 33131

keyAdmissions.admission: Witness was aware of problems around August 2022 regarding membrane application before concrete cured.
It was part of his duties to verify that identified defects were corrected.
He observed an installation issue with waterproofing in August 2022.
Schedule pressure was a factor in the early application of waterproofing.
The work was necessary and was performed.
Did not write a non-conformance report or verify remediation.

keyAdmissions.topic: Failure to Document Known Installation Defect
keyAdmissions.admission: Did not document a known installation defect with a non-conformance report for the first time.

keyDenials.denial: The condition shown in the photographs is not consistent with a properly installed waterproofing system.
Wouldn't characterize remediation as due to defective installation by a subcontractor.

inconsistencies.description: Witness initially states he verbally reported waterproofing issues to project manager Tom Brennan and noted it in his daily log (Page 5). He later admits he neither wrote a non-conformance report nor personally verified corrections (Page 5-6). These actions conflict with the responsibility of maintaining official records of defect reporting and ensuring corrections.

On page 7, Mr. Castillo testified that he first became aware of the balcony slab issues in August 2022. However, on pages 7-8, when Plaintiff's Exhibit 2 showed RFI No. 47 dated June 3, 2022 seeking clarification on the same issue, he acknowledged knowing about a detail conflict in June 2022. These statements conflict about when he first knew of the waterproofing issue.

Mr. Castillo initially claimed the remediation work was necessary (Page 11-12), but later admitted he did not personally inspect it and relied on payment records for confirmation (Page 15). These statements conflict about the basis of confirming remediation.

commonGrounds: Form, Calls for a legal conclusion

He acknowledged being aware of the membrane application problems by August 2022 but did not document the defects properly, as required by his role. Despite admitting that schedule pressures led to early application of waterproofing, Mr. Castillo failed to file a non-conformance report and verify the corrective actions. He also noted discrepancies regarding his awareness and reporting of the issues, with conflicting statements about the timeline and verification of remediation work. Furthermore, he did not agree with characterizations implying defective subcontractor installation.

summary: This document is a daily superintendent log for the Bayshore Tower Condominium project on August 12, 2022. It provides details on the construction work performed, crew on site, equipment usage, schedule status, issues observed, and deliveries. Notably, the project is behind schedule, and there is an issue with Coastal applying a waterproofing membrane prematurely.

keyIssues.title: Waterproofing membrane applied too early
keyIssues.description: Coastal Waterproofing applied the membrane on floors 12 and 13 before the concrete cure time met the 28-day requirement or 75% design strength, potentially violating specifications per RFI No. 47.

keyIssues.title: Project delay
keyIssues.description: The project is tracking approximately three weeks behind the baseline schedule, necessitating extended work hours for recovery.

summary: This document is a Request for Information (RFI) from Meridian Construction Group, regarding a conflict in the waterproofing detail for balcony-to-wall transitions between architectural and manufacturer's requirements at Bayshore Tower Condominium. The architect's response indicates that the manufacturer's guidelines should govern, confirming substrate curing requirements and superseding the architectural detail.

keyIssues.title: Waterproofing Detail Conflict
keyIssues.description: Conflict between architectural detail and manufacturer's requirements for balcony-to-wall transition waterproofing, impacting installation procedures.

extractedFacts.fact: Architectural detail (A-501) shows a 4-inch upturn and exposed termination.
Manufacturer's requirements include a minimum 6-inch upturn with concealed termination.
Concrete substrates must cure for a minimum of 28 days or reach 75% design strength before membrane application.
Response provided by Margaret Harlan, AIA, on June 13, 2022.

timeline.description: Scheduled start of waterproofing work on floors 11-14.
Architect responded confirming manufacturer's requirements should govern.
RFI No. 047 issued regarding waterproofing detail conflict.

summary: The document pertains to a construction dispute between Scherer Construction, LLC and Hedquist Construction, Inc., centered on alleged breaches of contract such as substandard asphalt paving work, breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, changes from asphalt to concrete paving, and responsibilities for repairs. The court addresses these issues, ultimately awarding Scherer withheld payments and remanding specific matters related to good faith for further consideration, highlighting disputes over repair obligations and contractual breaches.

keyIssues.title: Breach of Contract - Failure to Perform
keyIssues.description: Allegations that Scherer failed to provide reasonable notice to Hedquist to repair defective work.

keyIssues.title: Improper Solicitation of Change Order - Cardinal Change Doctrine
keyIssues.description: Claim that Hedquist improperly solicited a change order from the City of Casper, resulting in a significant change in project terms.

keyIssues.title: Change in Material - Asphalt to Concrete
keyIssues.description: Change in paving material from asphalt to concrete without proper notice, leading to a reduction in the subcontract value and allegations of breach of contract.

keyIssues.title: Allegation of Deficient Asphalt Work
keyIssues.description: Hedquist alleged Scherer's asphalt work was defective, citing deficiencies in density and thickness, leading to a dispute over responsibility and resulting set-off claims.

keyIssues.title: Denial of Reasonable Time for Repairs
keyIssues.description: The court found Hedquist failed to give Scherer reasonable time to address alleged defects, resulting in a denial of Hedquist's set-off request.

keyIssues.title: Breach of Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
keyIssues.description: The document discusses whether a breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing occurred in the context of a contract. It explores distinctions between tort-based and contract-based claims and the enforceability of this covenant in the context of commercial contracts.

keyIssues.title: Breach of Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
keyIssues.description: The document discusses whether a breach of the covenant of good faith and fair dealing can be claimed in Wyoming, highlighting specific cases where this was acknowledged or rejected by the courts.

keyIssues.title: Employment vs Commercial Contracts
keyIssues.description: The document distinguishes between the application of the implied covenant in employment contracts, where termination clauses are prevalent, and commercial contracts covered by the Uniform Commercial Code.

keyIssues.title: Cardinal Change Dispute
keyIssues.description: Scherer alleges that a cardinal change occurred when Hedquist was involved in a significant alteration of the contract terms that affected the material nature of the work.

keyIssues.title: Withholding Payments for Defective Work
keyIssues.description: Hedquist withheld payments from Scherer alleging non-compliance with contractual terms for defect correction and alleged failure to perform work in a timely manner.

keyIssues.title: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
keyIssues.description: The document identifies a dispute over whether Hedquist failed in the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in the contract with Scherer.

extractedFacts.fact: Scherer Construction, LLC was a subcontractor for a project involving East Second Street Reconstruction in Casper.
Hedquist Construction, Inc. was the main contractor for the East Second Street Reconstruction project.
The subcontract value was reduced from $448,240.45 to $105,098.81 due to a change in materials from asphalt to concrete.
The original subcontract called for the use of performance graded asphalt, which was later changed to concrete.
Scherer expended over $35,000 on special equipment and materials for asphalt paving which became superfluous due to the change to concrete.
Dispute over responsibility for substandard work in March 1999.
Wyoming recognizes a claim for breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under contract law.
The district court granted summary judgment in favor of Scherer, which is being contested based on the existence of the implied covenant.
In Wyoming, the at-will employment doctrine allows termination for any or no reason unless otherwise specified by contract.
Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205 is often referenced for the covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
Case references: Cody Country v. Chamber, Bourgeous v. Horizon Healthcare Corp.
Scherer was required to respond to the demand by July 5.
Hedquist was entitled to withhold payment from Scherer for defect correction costs under the subcontract.
The district court concluded that Hedquist did not provide reasonable notice to Scherer for defect correction.
Hedquist and Scherer are parties involved in the construction contract dispute.
The district court's order is partially affirmed and partially reversed for reconsideration.

timeline.description: Case of Husman v. State Farm Automobile Insurance Company mentioned.
Four Nines Gold, Inc. v. 71 Construction, Inc. case discussed.
Case mentioned: Astec Gas & Oil Company v. Roemer Oil Corporation.
Hoffman v. Bachmeier decision regarding summary judgment and tort-based theory was cited.
Court hearing on the dispute.
Hedquist sent a written demand to Scherer to correct defects.
Scherer filed an action against Hedquist claiming breach of subcontract and implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
Judgment for Scherer issued.
Deadline for Scherer to respond to Hedquist's demand.
Scherer did not respond by this date, prompting Hedquist to make arrangements for repairs.
City Engineer and Public Services Director recommended substituting concrete for asphalt paving, and a change order was approved.
Hedquist sent a letter to the Project Engineer detailing a cost comparison between concrete and asphalt.
Wyoming Supreme Court heard arguments in the dispute between Scherer Construction, LLC and Hedquist Construction, Inc.
Dispute over asphalt deficiencies reported.
Document creation discussing implications of legal precedents on the contract dispute.
District court's order on summary judgment was rendered and is now being reconsidered.
